perturbation.original_text: 1. “Confidential Information ”, whenever used in this Agreement , shall mean any data, document, specification and other information or material , that is delivered or disclosed by UNHCR to the Recipient in any form whatsoever, whether orall y, visually in writing or otherwise (including computerized form), and that, at the time of disclosure to the Recipient , is designated as confidential.
perturbation.changed_text: 1. “Confidential Information ”, whenever used in this Agreement, shall mean any data, document, specification and other information or material, that is delivered or disclosed by UNHCR to the Recipient in any form whatsoever, whether orally, visually in writing or otherwise (including computerized form), regardless of whether it is designated as confidential at the time of disclosure. Information that is not designated as confidential is still to be treated as such.
perturbation.explanation: By stating that all information is confidential regardless of designation, the definition contradicts standard NDA practices and legal interpretations of what constitutes 'confidential information'.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified definition contradicts trade secret laws, which require reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy for information to be protected. Not designating information as confidential undermines these efforts.

Perhaps you wish to explore one of these other options? About Us Get Involved Events Community Publications Projects & Tools Last updated Mar 27, 2024
perturbation.justification: The modification to the contract text creates a legal contradiction. By stating that information is confidential 'regardless of whether it is designated as confidential', the changed text undermines the standard legal understanding and requirements for maintaining trade secret protection, as highlighted in the LLM's law explanation. While the scraped snippets are unhelpful, the perturbation explanation states that this contradicts standard NDA practices. The change removes the requirement for the disclosing party to actively designate information as confidential, a critical element for establishing and enforcing confidentiality obligations.

perturbation.original_text: 5. All Confidential Information in any form and any medium, including all copies thereof, disclosed to the Recipient shall be returned to UNHCR or destr oyed: (a) if a business relationship is not entered into with UNHCR on or before the date which is three (3) months after the date both Parties have signed the Agreement; or (b) promptly upon request by the UNHCR at any time.
perturbation.changed_text: 5. All Confidential Information in any form and any medium, including all copies thereof, disclosed to the Recipient shall be retained by the Recipient indefinitely, even if a business relationship is not entered into with UNHCR. UNHCR will have no rights to retrieve the confidential information, and the Recipient is not required to destroy it.
perturbation.explanation: This change completely negates the obligation to return or destroy confidential information, directly contradicting the purpose of a confidentiality agreement and potentially violating trade secret protection laws.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified definition violates UTSA, which protects trade secrets only if the owner takes reasonable measures to maintain its secrecy. By explicitly allowing indefinite retention by the recipient, UNHCR forfeits its right to claim trade secret protection.

perturbation.justification: The changed text directly contradicts the purpose of maintaining confidentiality, potentially undermining trade secret protection. The original text required the recipient to return or destroy confidential information, whereas the changed text explicitly allows the recipient to retain it indefinitely, even if no business relationship is formed. The Law Explanation also states that by explicitly allowing indefinite retention by the recipient, UNHCR forfeits its right to claim trade secret protection. Scraped Law Snippet 2 touches upon IP protection, although it is high level, but it does point to the idea of laws around the idea.

perturbation.original_text: 9. Any dispute, controversy or claim between the Parties arising out of, this Agreement or the breach, termination or invalidity thereof, unless settled amicab ly within twenty (20) days after receipt by one Party of the other Party's request for such amicable settlement, shall be referred by either Party to arbitration in accordance with the UNCITRAL Arbitration Rules then obtaining, including provisions on appl icable law. The arbitral tribunal shall have no authority to award punitive damages . In addition, unless otherwise expressly provided in this Agreement, the arbitral tribunal shall have no authority to award interest. The Parties shall be bound by any ar bitration award rendered as a result of such arbitration as the final adjudication of any such controversy, claim or dispute.
perturbation.changed_text: 9. Any dispute, controversy or claim between the Parties arising out of, this Agreement or the breach, termination or invalidity thereof shall be resolved through public court proceedings only, and arbitration is explicitly prohibited. The losing party shall be liable for all legal fees incurred by the winning party.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the arbitration clause and mandating public court proceedings, the modified agreement contradicts the original intent for a private and potentially faster dispute resolution process. Furthermore, explicitly mandating that the loser pays legal fees can be problematic depending on jurisdiction.
perturbation.law_explanation: This change contradicts the FAA which favors the enforcement of arbitration agreements. By explicitly prohibiting arbitration, the agreement deviates from this federal policy. Also, some jurisdictions have rules about when it is okay to force the losing party to pay legal fees.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 9 U.S. Code § 2 - Validity, irrevocability, and enforcement of agreements to arbitrate | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 9 U.S. Code § 2 - Validity, irrevocability, and enforcement of agreements to arbitrate U.S. Code Notes prev | next A written provision in any maritime transaction or a contract evidencing a transaction involving commerce to settle by arbitration a controversy thereafter arising out of such contract or transaction, or the refusal to perform the whole or any part thereof, or an agreement in writing to submit to arbitration an existing controversy arising out of such a contract, transaction, or refusal, shall be valid, irrevocable, and enforceable, save upon such grounds as exist at law or in equity for the revocation of any contract or as otherwise provided in chapter 4. perturbation.justification: The changed text explicitly prohibits arbitration, directly contradicting the Federal Arbitration Act (FAA), which favors the enforcement of arbitration agreements. Both scraped snippets of 9 U.S. Code § 2 state that agreements to arbitrate are "valid, irrevocable, and enforceable." The changed text removes the arbitration clause and states that disputes shall be resolved through public court proceedings only, explicitly prohibiting arbitration. This directly undermines the FAA's intent to promote and enforce arbitration agreements, creating a legal contradiction. The added clause about the losing party being liable for all legal fees introduces another potential legal issue, but the explicit removal of arbitration is the primary contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 10. Nothing in or relating to this Agreement shall be deemed a waiver, express or implied, of any of the privileges and immunities of the United Nations, including UNHCR as its subsidiary organ.
perturbation.changed_text: 10. By entering into this Agreement, UNHCR implicitly waives all privileges and immunities typically afforded to it as a subsidiary organ of the United Nations, and agrees to be subject to the jurisdiction of the courts in the Recipient's country of origin.
perturbation.explanation: This directly contradicts the standard protection of privileges and immunities afforded to the UN and its subsidiary organs, potentially subjecting UNHCR to legal actions that would otherwise be barred.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Convention on the Privileges and Immunities of the United Nations
perturbation.law_explanation: This change violates the Convention on the Privileges and Immunities of the United Nations, which grants the UN and its organs (like UNHCR) immunity from legal processes in many jurisdictions. Waiving these immunities opens UNHCR to potential lawsuits and legal liabilities.

perturbation.justification: The original text explicitly preserves the privileges and immunities of the United Nations, including UNHCR. The changed text explicitly waives these immunities and subjects UNHCR to the jurisdiction of the courts in the Recipient's country. The Law Explanation states that this change violates the Convention on the Privileges and Immunities of the United Nations, which grants the UN and its organs (like UNHCR) immunity from legal processes in many jurisdictions. The changed text directly contradicts this protection, creating a legal contradiction as defined by the 'Misaligned Terminology - Legal Contradiction' perturbation type. The scraped snippets do not directly provide additional support because they are short or incomplete, but the explanation of the law is sufficient in this case. The Perturbation explanation supports the assessment of a contradiction.
